claimText: 1st Generation biofuels are produced using edible feedstocks such as wheat, corn, sugarcane, and food-grade oils.
evidenceExcerpt: 1st Generation uses edible feedstocks like wheat, corn, sugarcane, and food-grade oils.
examUse: Statement-based question on the classification of biofuels based on feedstock.
distractorHooks: Confusing 1G feedstocks with 2G feedstocks like Jatropha

claimText: 2nd Generation biofuels utilize non-edible lignocellulosic biomass and waste materials, including Jatropha, animal fats, and used cooking oil.
evidenceExcerpt: 2nd Generation utilizes lignocellulosic biomass and waste, such as wheat bran, animal fats, used cooking oil, and Jatropha.
examUse: Correct-pair question matching biofuel generations with their respective feedstocks.
distractorHooks: Classifying Jatropha as a 1st Generation feedstock
Stating 2G biofuels are derived from microalgae

claimText: 3rd Generation biofuels are derived from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass.
evidenceExcerpt: 3rd Generation is derived from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass.
examUse: Statement-based question distinguishing 3rd Generation biofuels from other generations.
distractorHooks: Claiming 3rd Generation biofuels use genetically engineered microorganisms

claimText: 4th Generation biofuels involve the use of genetically engineered microorganisms such as Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
evidenceExcerpt: 4th Generation involves genetically engineered microorganisms like Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
examUse: Statement-based question on the advanced technologies used in 4th Generation biofuels.
distractorHooks: Stating 4th Generation biofuels are derived from naturally occurring algae
